checkpoints.checkpointText: Also known as ir-Ramla taz-Zejtun, offers a mix of sandy shores and flat, rocky areas. You may also find SeaSport providers - one of the best locations for kayaking to explore the nearby sea caves and cliffs.

Artillery battery built by Knights of St. John in 1714-1716 to defend St. Thomas Bay. Known also as Ducluseaux Battery, possibly after the French knight Ducluseaux de Handessus. Part of it (its redan) has collapsed into the sea due to coastal erosion. Pass by the gate and the battery to follow this path
Natural limestone arch sculpted over centuries by sea winds and erosion. An iconic landmark of the Munxar path
Popular trekking landmark soft with spectacular views of light-colored limestone cliffs. Proceed with caution along the cliff edge.

checkpoints.id: Kappella u Torri ta' San Pawl Nawfragu
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Kappella u Torri ta' San Pawl Nawfragu
checkpoints.checkpointText: We have now entered the area known as Xrobb l-Ghagin and passing by a tower used for residential purposes with an adjacent chapel dedicated to the Shipwreck of St. Paul built in the 18th century. At the cross roads we will first go to the left to explore Xrobb l-Ghagin. After you take the left bend look back to see the chapel.

Area name literally meaning `the squeezing of the dough` for the fragile geological composition. We will first go downhill passing by the Xrobb l-Ghagin Sustainability Centre (Picnic area with WCs) for more views of the Hofra il-Kbira and then come back up here to take a side path onto the cliff side

We will be visiting two wonderful limestone cliffed bays known as il-Hofra il-Kbira (the large hole) and il-Hofra iż-Żgħira (the small hole). Observe the small natural arch in between the two bays. Take the stepped path down to the bay for am amazing sea level and cliff side views to it.
We have walked along a small promontory known as Ras il-Fenek where we can get spectacular views of both the Hofriet Bays on either side of it.

A polygonal fort built by the British between 1879 and 1883. Its primary function was as a fire control point controlling the massed guns of Fort Delimara on the headland below. Since 1991, the fort has been rented to the Island Sanctuary as a refuge for dogs. Nice grassed areas around the Fort's ditch which makes an ideal picnic spot

Built between 1881 and 1886 by the British to help Fort Tas-Silġ cover the defence of St Thomas Bay. You can take a small semi-circular walk around the D-shaped battery with three gun emplacements. Can also can serve as a great picnic spot!
A popular recreational area with a small sandy beach highly frequented by locals to swim and relax during the hot summer months, especially for young children.

We could only end the trek with more amazing views of white cliffed seas overlooking St. Thomas Bay. There is a stepped way down to get to sea-level and to a deep sea swimming spot which you may want to keep in mind for next summer

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts from near St. Thomas Bay Marsascala. Bus Route 91 from Valletta stops near the bay (92 and 93 to other Marsascala locations) or look to park yout vehicle along the St. Thomas Bay promenade. Starting coordinates: 35.855626, 14.566833

highlights.title: White Cliffs and Spectacular Bays
highlights.description: Starting from St. Thomas Bay the route first takes the renowned Munxar path over the white limtestone cliffs and then heads to Marsaxlokk Xrobb l-Ghagin and tas-Silg areas for two of the most spectacular bays in the Maltese Islands - il-Hofra il-Kbira and il-Hofra iż-Żgħira.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Large parts on off the beaten track for which trekking shoes are highly recommended for comfort and safety.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Parts of this trek are along cliff edges. Keep focus and caution

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: WCs near start and end points and half way through during the opening hours of the Xrobb l-Ghagin Sustainability Centre. Plenty of restaurants and snack bars at the starting and ending destinations. Various seasport providers at St. Thomas Bay.

treks.name: Delimara Bays
treks.shortDescription: Exploring wonderful bays and fortifications along the Delimara peninsula

checkpoints.checkpointText: As we walk past the Tourist Office where you can gather further information on the area during opening hours, we are walking along the streets where the Sunday fish market is held and then walk close to a small sandy beach known as tal-Maghluq

Now standing alongside the outer walls of Forti tas-Silg - a 19th-century polygonal fort built by the British to defend the harbour. After the uphill efforts we will get rewarded with great views onto Marsaxlokk harbour over the next part of the trek.

This pillbox forms part of the Wolesley Battery (behind the pillbox but not accessible), a coastal fortification built by the British in the late 19th century to defend strategic maritime approaches. Designed to support artillery positions, it reflects the shift toward reinforced concrete defenses and coordinated coastal surveillance. We will now take the road passing alongside it.
Amazing spot for a view over one of the most idyllic bays in Malta, known as il-Hofra iz-Zghira. Make sure to also do our Hofriet trek - one of the best treks discovering other such beauty.

A naturally carved limestone inlet, this pool is known for its flat rock ledges and remarkably clear, deep water shaped by centuries of wave action. Its sheltered formation creates calm conditions, making it a popular spot for swimming and cliff diving.

We have just walked alongside Malta's main power station, which has been in operation since 1992 and is a key part of Malta's energy infrastructure. Head straight on the main road and then we'll later return to this point via an alternative coastal path

Built by the British in the late 19th century, this polygonal fort was designed to guard key coastal approaches with heavy artillery and thick defensive walls. It later gained strategic importance as the site of a large coastal gun emplacement, reflecting advances in long-range naval defense. Take the adjcacent road along the Fort for a unique view onto Marsaxlokk harbour

First lit in the mid-19th century, this lighthouse was built by the British to guide vessels navigating the busy sea routes nearby. Its elevated position and powerful beam made it a crucial navigational aid, later modernized as maritime technology evolved.

A change of scenery into a raw and secluded coastal setting which is quite opposite to the area's name -  Xifer l-Infern (Hell's Edge). You will observe that it hosts one of the major Salt Pan area, the Devil's Edge Rock and further on also a small bay inlet named il-Kalanka tal-Gidien.

A small, secluded inlet shaped by natural erosion, this bay is framed by smooth limestone shelves that slope gently into clear, deep water. We have now visited all the major landmarks of this route and can head back to the end point - take the marked route on the map alongside the bay if accessible. If not accessible due to ongoing works, you can walk back opposite the bay to Fort Delimara and take the main road back. Before Fort tas-Silg we take a downhill path back to Marsaxlokk harbour

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route start and end points are at the popular Marsaxlokk Bay. There is a small parking lot near the Marsaxlokk Tourist Office. Bus routes 81 and 85 take from Valletta to Marsaxlokk. Area is very busy on Sunday mornings for the highly popular fish market. Start location coordinates: 35.841235, 14.545254

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: Starts from Marsaxlokk bay and heads uphill to the Delimary peninsula in search of iconic popular and less known bays, as well as some interesting fortifications and structures. The route ends at the other side of Marsaxlokk bay for some more serene views over the bay.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: For the major part on asphalted roads but also taking some rocky/ soily paths for which trekking shoes are recommended. Moderate elevation changes.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Some parts of this trek takes to cliff edges and areas subject to coastal erosion. Take caution and avoid going to extreme edges.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Marsaxlokk bay is a highly touristic area with plenty of restaurants, coffee shops and other amenities. Tourist Office where you can gather further information is located near starting point, with adjacent WC facilities.

checkpoints.checkpointText: Description....
Glimpse of World War 2 Pillbox fortification used to host infantry men with machine gun fire.
Has Saptan is characterised by a series of freshwater rockpools within Lower Coralline Limestone hosting rare species like the Tadpole Shrimp and the Painted Frog.
Info on the house
Hope you enjoyed this walk overlooking Wied Zembaq over its East side. Now we will be taking a path on your left side - look for rocks forming natural steps to a perpendicular path higher up.

Historical info on the wall. There are also Perhistoric temple remains cared after by Heritage Malta. After the trek you may wish to by a combo ticket to vist this site togeteher with Ghar Dalam Cave (500,000 year old pre-historic cave) and Museum. Tickets from Heritage Malta site or Ghar Dalam Museum.
A peacuful haven for tranquility and Catholic prayer dedicated to St. Mary
A small sandy beach with views towards Marsaxlokk. Enjoy the final stroll along the promenade and traditional Maltese boards ending our trek at Pretty Bay

The chapel dedicated to Saint George predates the surrounding fortifications (known to be already existing in 16th century), originally serving as a rural place of worship for the area. In the early 18th century, the Order of Saint John incorporated it into a redoubt, building defensive walls around the structure as part of a wider coastal defense network.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts from the San Pietru fil-Ktajjen Birzeubbuga Parish church and ends at nearby Pretty Bay. From Valletta you can tae bus routes 80 or 82. Starting coordinates: 35.827314, 14.527435

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: Will take us over and into a number of valleys - Valleys Zembaq, Saptan and Dalam, with the highlight being reaching id-Dar ta Pultu nestled within the valleys.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Relatively easy terrain with a mix of asphalted road and off the beaten track. Trekking shoes recommended, and necessary if terrain may not be dry

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Public toilets present at Pretty Bay which is the end destination (close to starting location). Plenty of restaurants and snack bars available near start and end locations too!

checkpoints.checkpointText: Info on the fortified wall
Farms & Farmhouses
Info on the Fort
On your right - largest solar farm in Malta. Small Island of Filfla ... protected nature reserve... we'll walk closer to it and other treks with a closer view
Look for and follow this path for a hidden gem discovery. Goes alongside cliff edges. Stay safe and do only what you're comfortable with!
Hidden Gem! You probably get why this cave gets it's name. Venture inside only if you feel you can do so safely
Find the path from the road to the cliffside path
Legend has it that ... Currently closed for safety reasons but can take a sneak peak
We'll next hike to Znuber valley. There are rock-hewn stairs leading to it. Follow them and stay safe!
Enoy the cliff-sided valley view onto the sea and the iconic stair path
Info on the Chapel
Recently opened park where you can enjoy and gather info on various tree species. Many picnic tables where you can take a snack and some rest before the final stretch back to Pretty Bay

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Bus routes and parking information. Coordinates to start point.

highlights.title: Cliffs, Caves & Znuber Valley
highlights.description: Benghisa countryside - Then... Details on the type of excursion.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Cliffside Paths & entering caves. Do only what you are comfortable and safe doing.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Terrain - Shoes. Weather conditions.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: WCs, picnic areas, restaurants and other facilities

checkpoints.checkpointText: Over this route you can find over 10 pillboxes as in the image. They are the last type of Malta's fortified coastal defences built by the British rule in WW2 and used to house infantrymen armed with small arms. Note the slits for small arms and machine gun fire. The letter P is first character for discount code
A deep sea bathing summer spot for locals. We will soon take a quick extra deviation with a path on the right alongside the next Pillbox paths for more coastal formations.
Coastal erosion at work, forming a mild cave formation. Avoid edges and going under the small caves/ rocks as this area is subject to heavy coastal erosions
A beautiful sea inlet - one of the many we'll observe along the route. Take a moment to enjoy the view.

Watchtower built in 1659 by Knights of St John Grand Master de Redin, as the 9th of the 13 de Redin towers. Note the single entrance on first floor that would have been accessible by a retractable rope or ladder. The number of stone layers before tower entrance are your next 2 characters for discount code
One of the flagship landmarks of the park. We will now take a detour to an uphill path to the right for more discoveries

Built in baroque style out of local limestone, presumably built to serve the religious needs of farmers who lived in the area, and it is sometimes known as Tas-Subriċint. Doorway is decorated with the coat of arms of Bishop Bartolomé Rull, surmounted by the arms of Grand Master Manuel Pinto da Fonseca. The marked year of completion make up your next 4 characters to discount code
You are in the quaint residential area of Zonqor. Letter Z is your next character for discount code. Small playground on your right if you would like a tranquil rest

A deep sea swimming zone, with numerous salt pans on the shore. Couple of restaurants and snack bars nearby. Look out for a particular colour patterned structure - count the number of layers and the digits are your next 2 characters for discount code
We're about to re-enter the official Inwadar Park area. WCs and small picnic area available before entrance.
BBQ Area on the right should you wish to return for a scenic BBQ. Straight ramp ahead for easier terrain - right one for a bit more rugged return to starting point.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts from the main bus station in Xghajra. Bus routes 94 from Valletta and 120 from Pembroke/ Mater Dei have this station as their destination. Plenty of parking spaces available along promenade. Start location coordinates: 35.887845, 14.549888

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: Takes us across and back the Inwadar National Park along the coast near ix-Xgħajra, Ħaż-Żabbar and Wied il-Għajn, offering a unique blend of history and nature experiences.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Mostly off the beaten track terrain with some rocky parts, but relatively easy. Comfortable trainers suffice in dry conditions but also consider trekking shoes for more comfort and safety.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Some parts of this trek takes to cliff edges and areas subject to coastal erosion. Take caution and avoid going to extreme edges.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: A restaurant is located near the start/ end locaton with nearby public toilets. The same applies half way through the trek. Also passess by small playgrounds and picnic table area for some rest/ packed lunch

treks.name: St. Lucija - Gudja Chapels
treks.shortDescription: Exploring field-nestled chapels and countryside paths

checkpoints.checkpointText: The first of several religious churches and chapels along this trek, this Santa Lucija parish church is dedicate to Pope Paul X. The first stone was laid on 6 November 1969 by Archbishop Mgr Michael Gonzi.
A short stroll along this pathway that offers vistas of the surrounding countryside and fields. The available benches offer the opportunity to soak in more the views
We will now take this path on the left to immerse more into the countryside and field paths.
This narrow countryside path with dense vegetation on both sides offers a unique experience worthy of an iconic landmark badge.
We have reached the end of this quiet countryside road. We will next head to Gudja village for which one needs to cross a busy road - use the pedestrian lights or crossings to do so safely.

You are seeing the beautiful facade of The Lieutenant Gardens that were built between 1800 to 1809 under Sir Alexander John Ball - the first British Governor of Malta & Gozo - as a sign of appreciation of Maltese sacrifice and bravery in forcing capitulation of the occupying French forces. The garden is privately owned but the path alongside it leads to a recently opened public Reading Garden with a hidden gem.

A 15th century medieval chapel dedicated to St. Mary, which had served as a parish church up to 1676. On the arrival of the Knights of St John, a captain was appointed to defend the area of Bir Miftuħ. In 1565, during the Great Siege of Malta, the church was desecrated by the invading Turks. It is said that in order to preserve the treasures of the church, including the bells, from the invaders, the people buried them in the tombs of the church.
Another scenic path that takes to the next field nestled chapel.

Built in 1676 giving thanks to Our Lady of Loreto for the deliverance of the plague which pestered the Maltese Islands that year. Was occupied by the military during World War II, connecting the well under the church to an underground shelter.

Roman Catholic church dedicated to the Assumption of St. Mary whose feast is celebrated on the 15th of August. Built mostly in Baroque style and built in 1656 on the plans of Tumas Dingli. Note the St. Mary and St. Joseph statues flanking the church - a common dual commemoration in the village you might have also observed in some of the countryside structures.
Built in 1754 on the site of an earlier medieval one. Note the statues of St. Joachim and St. Anne sculpted on the faceade.

This final chapel in the village of Gudja represents a typical example of 17th century ecclesiastical architecture, rectangular in shape and characteristics water sprouts. Internal altar piece in the centre of the apse depics the martyrdom of St. Catherine. We will now head to the earlier countryside path in reverse direction to St. Lucija
Taking from the other end the field side promenade in St. Lucija before heading slightly uphill to the final destination

Our trek ends at the lovely Chinese Garden of Serenity - a very peaceful garden and also symbolises yin and yang, life from birth to death. The garden has fountains, a zig zag formed corridors, bridges, different types of doors with their own specific meaning - a perfect setting for a rest at the end of a long trek.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Bus routes 80 and 83 serve St. Lucija from Valletta bus terminus. Village offers plenty of private vehicle parking spaces. Starting coordinates: 35.863554, 14.505095

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: Route takes us to several churches and chapels, mainly in Gudja two of which are beautifully nestled within countryside fields. Route starts from the centre of St. Lucija village and ends at the Chinese Garden of Serenity in same village.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Almost entirely on asphalted roads and with little elevation gain. Can be done in comofortable trainers.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Route passes along a number of benches and small gardens/ playgrounds where one can take a break with a snack. WC facilities near starting location, within main Gudja playground and the end Chinese Garden of Serenity playground

checkpoints.checkpointText: During opening hours it is worth visiting this beautifully designed garden dedicated to renowned singers of traditional Maltese folk music (Ghana) from Zejtun. One can find statues commemorating these singers, a kids playground and a wide vista over Zejtun fields up to St. THomas Bay in Marsascala.

From inside the playground one could also get an outside look to this garden commissioned by the first British Governor of Malta Sir Alexander Ball for the enjoyment of the village captains nominated by the British. We have encountered other similar gardens and facades in some of our other treks.

This countryside road taking to Marsaxlokk has numerous side alleys. If you want a small detour to venture into one of them, we have just arrived to one of the most characteristic - A dead-end alley with green fields and low-lying farm structures that takes close to the site of the British Battery of Mistrah Strejnu (properly visible from the other side).

A remotely located chapel within a scenic setting that was rebuilt in 1640 in an architectural style typical of the 17th century. Historic records of this chapel data back to the start of the 16th century. The feast of St. Nicholas is celebrated in this chapel yearly on the 6th of December.

On either side of the road you can observed excavated remains of a multi-period sanctuary site with over 4,000 years of history. Overlooking Marasaxlokk bay, the site originated as a Neolithic megalithic temple (around 3000 BC), was adapted by the Phoenicians for hosting a temple dedicated to the goddess Astarte, and later served as a Roman sanctuary for Juno before being replaced by a Byzantine monastery.

Dedicated to our Lady of the Snows, this church was originally built in 1650 by a well to-do family. Was used by the military during WW2 and today the convent has been turned into a religious retreat house. Our Lady of the Snows? originates from a 4th-century Roman legend, where, during the night of August 5, 352 (or 358) A.D., a miraculous, unseasonal snowfall covered the Esquiline Hill in Rome. It is definitely one of the churches with the most amazing scenery. Once having taken in the views onto Delimara hills, we can take the path on the side of the church for another amazing view.
This quite hidden spot offers an amazing open view onto Delimara promontory and Marsaxlokk Bay. Areas we will discover more fully in one of our other treks.
Path back to Zejtun
checkpoints.badgeImage: This path will lead us back to Zejtun. Along the way some dogs might follow and bark at you. Harmless, but if this can panic you, one can still take the path back in reverse direction and rejoin the designed route at a later point.

checkpoints.checkpointText: Dedicated to St. Catherine of Alexandria, although it is more commonly known as the church of St. Gregory due to the fact that the famous annual procession of St. Gregory held on the first Wedensday after Easter ends here with a celebrated mass (traditionally starting from Mdina but now confined to a shorter route within Zejtun. It is one of the oldest churches in Malta dating before the 15th century, which is also marked by the more fortified looking architectural style. Our trek ends here, where you can also observed a statue dedicated to St. Gregory in the middle of the nearby parking lot.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The route starts adjacent to a public garden dedicated to Ghannejja (Maltese folk singers) from Zejtun. Served by bus 84 from Valletta to 'Girgor' bus stop. Private vehicle parking spaces available on the opposite side of the garden, near the old parish church. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.852771, 14.538642.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: Takes us along countryside roads and paths to Marsaxlokk and back, encountering various historical landmarks and scenic views along the way. Taking a small loop then it returns close to the starting point at the nearby Zejtun Old Parish church dedicated to St. Gregory.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Along asphalted roads with little elevation change. Can be done in comfortable trainers.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: When taking a different path back from Marsaxlokk to Zejtun, some dogs might follow and bark at you. Harmless, but if this can panic you, one can still take the path back in reverse direction and rejoin the designed route at a later point.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Public WC facility within the public garden at route start & end.

checkpoints.checkpointText: Hope you are enjoying one of the most spectacular views of the steep Wied Babu cliffs leading to the sea. Now look for a path over the small wall that actually leads us there over rugged terrain.
Congratulations for reaching this amazing spot - a memorable experience worth a hidden gem badge.

A striking natural arch carved into the limestone cliffs, this formation is part of a network of sea caves shaped by centuries of wave erosion. The way sunlight reflects off the seabed and into the cave creates vivid blue hues that shift throughout the day. Worth taking a boat trip to experience it at sea-level - the adjacent downhill road leads to the shore where one can take such rides on traditional Maltese boats.

Elevated view over Wied iz-Zurrieq bay inlet - This narrow inlet cuts deep into the limestone coastline, formed by the persistent action of waves exploiting natural fissures in the rock. Its steep cliffs and sheltered waters have long provided a natural landing point and access to nearby sea caves.

We are now the Mnajdra and Hagar Qim temples and Archeological Complex, and at the starting point of another of our trekking routes that leads from here to Lapsi bay. For this particular trek, we take the road on the right that takes us the charismatic village of Qrendi.

Dating back to the 17th century, this chapel is dedicated to Saint Matthew and is closely linked to the nearby Maqluba sinkhole we will be visiting next. Its simple Baroque design and enduring presence reflect both religious devotion and the cultural significance of the surrounding landscape.

A large sinkhole formed by the sudden collapse of limestone bedrock. Its steep sides and lush interior create a unique microhabitat, contrasting sharply with the surrounding terrain. Local tradition recounts a dramatic collapse in 1343, when the ground suddenly gave way following a violent storm, swallowing buildings that once stood on the site. According to legend, only a small chapel dedicated to Saint Matthew remained untouched (behind the current church which we'll get to next), reinforcing the area’s strong religious associations.

This chapel was built in 1650 and is dedicated to Our Lady of Mercy, traditionally invoked for protection from illness and misfortune. It became a focal point for local devotion, particularly during times of hardship, and is associated with annual celebrations that reflect enduring community traditions.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The route starts and concludes at the Zurrieq main road leading to Blue Grotto. Plenty of parking spots avaialble on the side roads. Bus route 74 connects Valletta to the starting location at stops 'Formosa' or 'Wied'. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.829129, 14.468738.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: Takes us along countryside roads and paths to Marsaxlokk and back, encountering various historical landmarks and scenic views along the way. Taking a small loop then it returns close to the starting point at the nearby Zejtun Old Parish church dedicated to St. Gregory.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: For the major part on asphalted roads and pavements, except for an excursion to reach the valley bed and sea shore of Wied Babu where the terrain is very rugged with strong elevation drops/ rises. Trekking shoes recommended for this part of the excursion.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: The excursion to Wied Babu is quite tortuous and rugged and call for special care. Always respect private land.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Some snackbar and take away outlets are located on the Zurrieq Main Road near the starting/ ending locations. Public WC facilities not directly on route, but available with diversions to Wied iz-Zurrieq (downhill road) or at the Mnajdra and Hagar Qim Archeological complex. Some of the most panoramic picnic tables in Malta available along the route.

checkpoints.checkpointText: Seaviews
Fort and Park
Dedicated to San Gakbu...
Looking over to Smart City

highlights.title: Test Route
highlights.description: To check if it works

treks.name: Tempji-Lapsi Trail
treks.shortDescription: Archeological Park with Prehistoric Temples to Pristine Bays

checkpoints.checkpointText: Buy tickets during from here to view artefacts and visit the Hagar Qim and Mnajdra Archeological Archeological Park. Buy tickets from here or a snack
Hagar Qim info - The first of the two... dated...
The second set of temples...
Monument dedicated to Sir Walter Congreve, a former Governor of Malta who upon his wish was buried at sea between this Monument and the island of Filfla

One of the 13 coastal towers built by the Knights of St John Grand Master de Redin. Built in 1658-1659 as part of a chain of coastal fortifications to protect agaist the threat of a Turkish invasion and corsairs

Now we head back to Congreve Memorial over the Garrigue landscape. Look for a steep path down on the left side of the memorial. Path is very steep so do only in safe conditions - get down on your bottocks if required for further safety!
Natural arch ....
The coastal stretch we will be following (higher up) is formed by several of such pretty small bays
Proceed with caution to discover this hidden small inlet sea formation
One of the most iconic bays in the Maltese Islands. Admire the cave formation enclosing the bay. Restaurant, restrooms and WC facilities
Lapsi Tower... You can get closer taking a side path
Info on the water carriage
Take a look at the sea valley formation whilst respecting private property
Only stretch of trek alongside road traffic... but wide street and named Panarama Road for a reason!

Look for this path on your right for a more scenic off the beaten trek to the Megalithic temples. You'll be walking in a Garigue (Xaghri) landscape characteristic of many parts of the island, consisting of exposed open spaces with rugged, rocky outcrops and dry shrubs which spring into life in Autumn. The most common hosted species is the Mediterranean Thyme whose perfume attracts many species, including the Maltese bee.
As we reach the temples and end of trek, take some time again to admire the view. Hope you enjoyed the trek!

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Bus routes and parking information. Coordinates to start point.

highlights.title: Archeological Park and then heading to hidden gems and landmarks along the Coast
highlights.description: Details on the type of excursion.

highlights.title: Another trek combining history and nature
highlights.description: The trail passes near ancient watchtowers and fortifications.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Terrain - Shoes. Weather conditions.

highlights.title: Safety Warnings
highlights.description: Terrain - Shoes. Weather conditions.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: WCs, picnic areas, restaurants and other facilities

treks.name: Girgenti-Laferla Cross
treks.shortDescription: Walking up to the Laferla Cross

checkpoints.checkpointText: Start of Wied Hesri
Wied Hesri
Girgenti Bridge
Madonna tal-Konsagrazzjoni Chapel
Wied Girgenti
Laferla Cross

checkpoints.id: Our Lady of Mt Carmel Cemetery
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Our Lady of Mt Carmel Cemetery
checkpoints.checkpointText: Our Lady of Mt Carmel Cemetery

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Bus routes and parking information. Coordinates to start point.

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: Details on the type of excursion.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Terrain - Shoes. Weather conditions.

highlights.title: Safety Warnings
highlights.description: Terrain - Shoes. Weather conditions.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: WCs, picnic areas, restaurants and other facilities

checkpoints.checkpointText: First glimples of the Wied Ghomor greener as we walk under a flyover road.
Take the road on the left for a path into the valley

You have reached the heart of the vallery and its greener vegetation. Enjoy the peaceful surroundings. You can venture further if feeling adventorous and conditions allow but this routes goes back along the same path we came from.
From the short flight of steps enjoy an elevated view of the Mensija Valley we'll be heading to next
Again surronded by vegetation, we are in the heart of the second valley of the trek - Wied Mensija.

A suggestive picnic area with views over the valley. A good spot for a snack break before we head back to the coast. We will next walk for a short while on a busy road. Take care, especially crossing the road
Ghomor Valley on one Side and Spinola Bay on the other, with the St. Julian's Parish church right in front. One of the more modern churches built in 1961 and visited by the Pope in 1989.
One of the most picturesque and iconic bays in Malta, with vibrant colours from the colourful luzzus and lively atmosphere. Picturesque in different ways during both the day and night

The small sandy beach overlooked by splendid Neo-Gothic facade of the Church of Our Lady of Mount Carmel, make this another iconic hotspot in the Maltese islands. The interior Gothic architecture of the church is equally impressive. This promenade stretch offers a magnificient relaxing spot to end the trek.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The route starts from the well known Spinola Bay, near the `LOVE` monument. Bus routes 13, 14 and 16 run from Valletta and Sliema to the bay. Routes 202, 212 and 222 arrive from Northern regions. Finding parking can be challenging - the Ta' Giorni area in nearby San Gwann is a quieter option and not too distant. Route ends in nearby Balluta Bay. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.9191, 14.4900.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: A scenic walk starting from coast and heading into the green lung of the area - Wied Ghomor and Mensija. Returns to the coastal promenade, ending at the picturesque Balluta bay.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: For the major part on asphalted roads, venturing into some valley paths. Can be done in trainers in dry conditions. Little elevation change.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: A small part of the route is along busy roads and contains a couple of busy road crossings. Take caution and use zebra crossing/ lights where possible

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: The starting and ending locations are at very touristic areas with a wide variety of tourist amenities. Half way through, the route passes by a quiet picnic area with tables - so bringing a packed lunch is also a good idea!

treks.name: Gharghur Victoria Lines Trail
treks.shortDescription: Victory Lines Trail and Valleys of Gharghur

checkpoints.checkpointText: A lovely and skilfully crafted minitiature statue of the Parish church of St. Bartholomew, that had characterised the trek starting point. Route now takes a scenic path overlooking Wied Anglu and reaching the area known as Birguma, before we actually had down into the valley along the Victoria Lines

Having reached the Birguma residential area with its villas, now look for a rightside path for the path along the Victoria Lines. Part of a 19th-century British defensive line stretching across the island, this path follows fortifications built to create a strategic land barrier. We will take the Gharghur part of the path with old walls, watch posts, and natural escarpments that once formed a continuous line of defense. Worth taking some safe lookbacks along the way for more scenic views. Take care.
Hope you enjoy the highly scenic experience of descending and ascending the Wied Anglu valleybed along the historic Victoria Lines. Here is an iconic landmark badge as a memory of the experience.

These rock-cut cave are believed to have been used since antiquity, taking advantage of the soft limestone to create simple yet effective shelters. Archaeological findings suggest periods of habitation and reuse, offering insight into early rural life and adaptation to the landscape. What a home view they used to have!

Hope you enjoyed the amazing wide vistas from the different viewpoints from this area known as 'Top of the Word'. Here is a main viewing point where you can also take a break with a view on the benches that overlook the Malta's North East area and on a clear day as far as Gozo and Comino. Right ahead you are looking at the Bahar ic-Caghaq coastal area. After a small break, the route continues along the Victoria Lines heading down another valley.

Again another snenic valley descent and ascent along the Victoria Lines, this time along Wied Faham. You are actually now standing within the San Giovanni Battery - a late 19th-century British military fortification completed in December 1898 it was designed to house quick-firing guns and machine guns, and featuring concrete platforms and an underground magazine.

If fatigue has not taken over, you can now take a small and quiet circular route around Fort Madliena and its ditch, and amongst surrounding trees. Constructed by the British in the late 19th century, Fort Madliena formed part of the Victoria Lines defensive system designed to divide and protect the island. Its polygonal layout, deep ditch, and caponier defenses reflect advances in military engineering of the period.
A highly picturesque and photographed spot, this is a charming stone bridge spanning the Wied id-Dis valley. Enjoy the views over the valley

A beuatiful chapel perched on a flight of steps facing scenic valley views, this is a locally cherished chapel formerly known as Santa Marija taz-Zellieqa chapel. Dating back to 1530 and rebult in 1650.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts from the Gharghur Parish church dedicated to St. Bartholomew and ends at a nearby valley area behind the church. Since finding parking near the churc is difficult, looking for parking in the residential area behind it provides better options. Bus routes 46 and 260 connect Valletta and Gharghur. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.923972, 14.453204.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: This route explores varuious valleys surrounding Gharghur (Wied Anglu, Wied Faham and Wied id-Dis), with a main section of it being along the Victoria Lines trail section for spanning from the Birguma area to Madliena.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Sections on the Victoria Lines path are quite rugged and involve descending and climbing a substantial number of high stepped stairs, and a significant elevation change within short distance. Trekking shoes are recommended for safety and comfort.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Take care along the Victoria Lines path as some sections edge over the valleys with no side walls. This route should be done in dry and non-windy conditions for safety, and not recommended for those with fear of heights.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Local band clubs and bars are located directly near the church square at the starting point, where you can enjoy a snack or a drink in these tranditional bars and which typically have restroom facilities available for patrons. The church itself has restroom facilities. Benches where one can rest with a view are avaialable at the Top of the World area, right in between the two main efforts of this trek.

treks.name: Wied il-Kbir & Wied Qirda
treks.shortDescription: The larget valley system in Malta

checkpoints.checkpointText: We are coming across the second small bridge over the valley bed. You can take the short path on the left amongst the trees in one direction and stick to the road when coming back the other direction.

You will come across some water management related structures as Wied il-Kbir houses one of Malta's most significant historical and modern water management systems. Originally developed in the late 19th century, it continues to be a critical node in the national network managed by the Water Services Corporation. The system was largely the brainchild of British engineer Sir Osbert Chadwick in the 1880s.
We will be using all the paths in this crossroads - take first the leftest one for a hidden gem discovery and then when coming back take the one straight ahead

ALso known as Ghar Tas-Slaleb because of the carved crosses at the start of the carve. Hence it is believed to have been used as a medieval church. Venture into only where you know you can safely return and with appropriate ligthing.
A nice small open area where you can sit on a rock to take in the surroundings and maybe a snack too before proceeding with more challenging parts of the trek

We will now take a scenic and more rugged path within the valley. So we will not have checkpoints for a while. There is mainly a single path along the route, with a single main cross path. Always stick to the one mostly to your right.
This elevated spot offers one of the most iconic viewpoints over the valley. Take some time to enjoy it and take it all in.

checkpoints.id: More Views on onto fields & valley
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: More Views on onto fields & valley
checkpoints.checkpointText: The quarry on left side is no great sight but on the otherside you can look over for some more amazing views over the fields and valleys. Also see if you can spot this traditional Maltese well.
Enjoy the amazing view of the steep valley along which we started the route and reached Ghar Hanzir. The earned badge will also keep this memory for you!
We will now trek a part of the Wied Qirda valley bed, which is narrower and its rock faces offer interesting formations. For instance look out for the following resemebling an elephant's head!

checkpoints.id: The Tomb of the English Lady
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: The Tomb of the English Lady
checkpoints.checkpointText: A mysterious rock-carved doorway believed by some to be a tomb, alhough no remains have been found within. thought to have been built by Major Patrick Yule of the Royal Engineers around 1842-43, possibly in memory of a Lycian expedition. Locals also associate it with the burial of an English woman who died in the area. You can proceed with trekking along this path through thicker vegetation and which takes you to the main upper path of Wied Qirda (explored in another route). This routes concludes by trekking back to our starting point.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Starting point most easily reached by private vehicle or ride share, with possibility of side road parking at the start of the valley. Otherwise, bus routes 73 and 74 from Valletta have 10-minute away stops. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.87060, 14.47625.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: The route is in the form of a semi-loop, starting and ending at the start of Wied il-Kbir Valley from Qormi side. Trekking along a large valley system we will explore Wied il-Kbir and Wied Qirda and explore some of their interesting geological formations and vegetation.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Large parts of the trek route goes along rugged and rockey paths, so trekking boots are necessary. This is also a main water catchemnt area that can hold water and muddy paths for several days. Elevation change is moderate.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Cave discovered along the way - venture only where you are sure you can safely return and with appropriate ligthing. Rocky paths can be slippery, even with trekking boots.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: This is a trek fully immersed in nature with no amenities or facilities along the way. So bring plenty of water and food for re-fuelling at some of the amazing open areas and viewpoints of this trek.

checkpoints.checkpointText: This small chapel is dedicated to Our Lady and reflects the long tradition of Marian devotion found in the Maltese countryside. Its name “tal-Blat” (“of the rock”) likely refers to the rocky ground or stone outcrop on which the chapel was built. It's lighting at night adds charm to the chapel.
You are now on a long path leading to Attard and overlooking the main Wied is-Sewda path. Enjoy the peaceful surroundings along various farmhouses and niches with statues dedicated to St. Mary

You will soon reach the end of this path after which we need to take a left for a very short stretch along a main road before taking again a left to enter the main Wied is-Sewda path whose entrance is displayed in the image.

Wied is-Sewda is one of the longest valleys on the island, cutting through the landscape as it carries seasonal rainwater toward the coast. Its name, meaning the black valley, may derive from the dark soil and shaded rock formations found along parts of its course.
Route now takes a detour to the uphill countryside roads that will lead us to the Tal-Hlas Chapel with some scenic view spots over the valley along the way

This chapel is dedicated to Our Lady of Deliverance (tal-Ħlas), reflecting a long-standing devotion to the Virgin Mary as a protector and intercessor in times of need. Its traditional stone architecture and rural setting echo the centuries-old practice of building small chapels as places for prayer and thanksgiving in the Maltese countryside.

This parish church, dedicated to St. George, was completed in the late 17th century and is one of Qormi's two historic parish churches. Designed in the Baroque style, it reflects the town’s growth during that period

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The trek starts towards the end of Qormi Valley Road and ends at the nearby San Gorg Parish church. Finding parking along Valley Road can be challenging, unless proceeding to its end. Bus routes 61 and 63 from Valletta get you close to the starting point. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.88308, 14.46882

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: The route is designed over paths in Wied is-Sewda that lies between the towns of Qormi, Attard and Zebbug. It fast takes a countryside path from Qormi to Attard overlooking the main Wied is-Sewda path which will be then taken in the reverse direction from Attard. From the main path we divert uphill to the tal-Hlas Chapel before heading to Qormi's historic town roads to conclude route at St. George Parish Church.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Although almost entirely on countryside paths, the route is fully asphalted roads so can be done in trainers. If on or following rainy days, trekking shoes are more adbvisable.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Some cafes and bars can be found near St. George Parish Church marking the end of the route. Qormi is renowned for its local bakeries and sweets shops - good places for a treat after the trek!

treks.name: Wied Qannotta - Ghajn Rihana
treks.shortDescription: For amazing green views with a touch of historic heritage

checkpoints.checkpointText: The trek starts from the Burmarrad Parish Church of the Immaculate Heart of Mary - a modern Roman Catholic sanctuary established in 1971 and managed by the Franciscan Conventual Friars.

archaeological site features the most extensive remains of a Roman agricultural villa ever discovered in Malta, including ancient olive-pressing equipment and fortifications. Managed by Heritage Malta, the site is traditionally believed to be where St. Publius welcomed St. Paul after his shipwreck, though it remains closed to the public except for special open days.

We now take the path to Wied Qannotta -  a scenic, fertile valley stretching between Burmarrad, Wardija and Bidnija. As a typical Maltese wadi, it serves as a natural watercourse during the rainy season, supporting local agriculture and vital groundwater collection.

The short uphill path has taken us this derelict 16th-century wayside chapel. Originally dedicated to the Nativity of Our Lady, it was rededicated to the Immaculate Conception in the 18th century following a rebuilding. Make sure to also take in the amazing views over the valley from this spot.
Take a moment to enjoy one of the greenest viewpoints in Malta, overlooking the fertile valley of Wied Qannotta nestled between the Wardija and Bidnija hills

The downhill path with the green views onlooking the Targa Gap ridge, has taken us to the chapel of St. Margaret. Though its exact origins are unknown, it was first documented in 1575 by Monsignor Pietro Dusina and later rebuilt in its current form in the 18th century.

We have now reached a recently regenerated path alongside the natural watercourse of Ghajn Rihana. The name likely refers to the myrtle plant (riħan), and the valley features a stream that flows for most of the year, particularly after the winter rains.

Hidden amongst the farmhouses lies the Tal Qadi Temple - a megalithic temple site dating back to the Tarxien phase (c. 3300–3000 BC). A famous globigerina limestone slab was discovered here in 1927, featuring incised star-like symbols and a crescent moon. Many scholars believe it represents one of the world's oldest lunar calendars or star maps.

We have now reached the most renowned Bird Park in Malta, as well as St. Michael Chapel. This is a late 16th-century wayside chapel, rebuilt in its present form in the 18th century. Characterised by simple facade and small bell-cot, standing as a historic landmark within the fertile Għajn Riħana valley.
Thanks for experiencing with us this trek, which now approaches the end through the quiet fields of the Burmarrad plain before reaching again the Burmarrad Parish Church.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Start and end from near Burmarrad Parish church. From Valletta, buses 41, 42, 45, 48, or 250 offer a direct route to the area. Vehicle parking best sought in side streets or proceeding further near Lidl supermarket.  GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.933550, 14.415173.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: From the hamlet of Burmarrad, the route heads into the scenic valley of Wied Qannotta, before looping back through the Ghajn Rihana valley whilst exploring scenic views and some hidden historic landmarks.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Within Wied Qannotta, the path is a mix of soily and rocky terrain, with some short uphill sections. Certain areas can also hold muddy waters. Trekking shoes are hence recommended. The rest of the route is largely on asphalted or level paths.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: One major road crossings but with pedestrian traffic lights available - use them for safety.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Food outlets and small bars/ pubs present in the centre of Burmarrad hamlet near the start and end points of the route. The rest of the route is immeersed in nature.

checkpoints.checkpointText: Recently opened trail along Fiddien Valley to Chadwick Lakes. The area got its name after the intervention of Sir Osbert Chadwick, when during the second half of the 19th century planned several dams, of various sizes and forms, with the intention to retain as much rainwater as possible.

You have now reached the main water catchment area of the Qlejgha Valley, forming the most searched scene  within the Chadwick lakes system. The stream then continues to meander around the valley floor, forming small pools, or even small waterfalls, wherever there are depressions in the ground. The amount of water present in the valley is very seasonal.

The uphill path on the left side takes us to the planned route to Bingemma but it is suggested to first continue for the rest of the road ahead to enjoy in full the Qlejgha valley, and then return to this mark.

The climb was worth it. Has taken us to this hlll known as il-Qolla with 360 degree scenic views. Venture into the rock formations of the hill for more amazing views. A great place to take a snack break in some shade provided by the trees.

Reaching near the Bingemma cliff edge, you can enjoy views over the Bingemma Gap with the Victoria Lines on the left, Our Lady of Itria chapel ahead, the town of Mgarr on the right and the Gozo channel ahead.

We have already been walking along the Victoria Lines - a 12km British line of fortifications along the width of Malta - but we have now reached one of its most scenic and photographed spots within the Bingemma Gap

checkpoints.id: Chapel of Our Lady of Itria
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Chapel of Our Lady of Itria
checkpoints.checkpointText: A humble rural chapel quietly overlooking the surrounding valleys. Built centuries ago by local farmers and travelers, it has long served as a peaceful waypoint for those crossing these Maltese hills.
A small countryside chapel that has watched over this path for generations. Pilgrims and villagers once stopped here to rest and pray, making it a quiet landmark along Rabat’s old rural routes.

As we reach the end of the route, we go over this bridge over Wied il-Fiddien which we had gone under in the first part of the Chadwick Lakes trail. Take a moment to again enjoy the serenity of the Fiddien Valley.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts and ends near entrance to Chadwick Lakes trail. By bus one can take routes 50, 51, 52, 53 from Valletta to Rabat (Stop at Saqqajja) and then change route to 109, and stop at Fiddien. By car there are limited parking spaces available near entrance - for less parking worries, leave vehicle near Għajn National Water Conservation Centre and take a 500m walk to start route. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.886291, 14.380207

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: First takes the recently regenerated and incredibly scenic path to Chadwick Lakes and walk within the Fidden and Qlejgha valleys, before heading up towards the Bingemma cliff viewpoints and Victoria lines at Bingemma Gap. A shorter return route is taken along vehicle roads surrunded by greenery.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Crossing Fiddien and Qlejgha valley paths, as well as rocky terrain at Bingemma Gap. Possible with trainer shoes in dry conditions but trekking shoes recommended.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Keep safe and do not take risks near Bingemma Cliff Edges.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: No restrooms or food outlets along the route, so think ahead and bring water and snacks with you. On Weekends, there are usually some stands with traditional Maltese snacks set up near Chadwick Lakes trail entrance.

checkpoints.checkpointText: Area known as tax-Xlejli in Gudja. Restroom facilties
Now moving to countryside roads. Although quite quiet, the streets are narrow so keep on the left side
In the distance over the fields you can spot the Ta Loretu chapel. Another landmark you can reach from the centre of Gudja on another trek
Enjoy an iconic view of this trek overlooking the Saptan Valley
One of the three semaphore towers in Malta. We are now in the village of Hal Ghaxaq

checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Tiny Park dedicated to Animal love
checkpoints.checkpointText: A small haven where you can take a break near the monument dedicated to animals and let the little ones name the animals
Enjoy more views over the agricultural fields

The Xlejli Tower also known as Bettina Tower now comes into view. It is thought to have served as a watchtower as far as from Roman or Medieval times but its exact age remains a mystery. It is located within a private Palazzo but you can get closer from the paths on your right

Roman Catholic church dedicated to the Assumption of St. Mary whose feast is celebrated on the 15th of August. Built mostly in Baroque style and built in 1656 on the plans of Tumas Dingli. The trek now closes at Gnien Raymond Caruana for a well deserved rest or kids play time.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts from the parish church of Gudja and ends at a nearby playground. Bus 88 from Valletta goes to start location. Most parking slots in tax-Xlejli area if by private transport. Start location coordinates: 35.8481865, 14.5027438

highlights.title: Type of Walk
highlights.description: Most of walk goes along countryside roads in Gudja and Ghaxaq to enjoy most of the nature views in this area.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Very flat route, most of it on tarmac roads with parts on a bit bumpier concrete paths.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: While light on traffic, vehicles can approach on some of the narrow countryside roads. Keep caution by sticking to left side of the road.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Route takes to a playground with restroom facilities a few minutes after the start, and ends in another public garden. Restaurant with Maltese cuisine near Parish church.
